reference_answer: FEI 2513595. Stated in the inspection paragraph immediately after the salutation and again in the instructions for the electronic reply at the end of the letter ("Identify your response with FEI 2513595…").
Francis Godwin, Director, Office of Manufacturing Quality, Office of Compliance, Center for Drug Evaluation and Research. From the first signature block at the end of the letter.
Warning Letter 320-26-61. Printed on its own line directly above "April 9, 2026" / "Dear Mr. Stone:".
VIA UPS. Listed in the header block at the top of the letter under "Delivery Method:".

The electronic reply must be sent to `CDER-OC-OMQ-Communications@fda.hhs.gov`. The response must identify FEI 2513595 and be addressed ATTN: Andrew Haack. Stated in the second-to-last paragraph before the signature blocks.

In the subsection titled "*Complaint Investigations*" under CGMP Violation #3 (21 CFR 211.192). The other two subsections in that violation are "*Finished Drug Product Microbiological Testing*" and "*Stability Assay*".
"Consultant Recommended". The order of headings near the end of the letter is: Responsibilities as a Contractor → Quality Systems → Consultant Recommended → Conclusion.

Footnote 1 (the only footnote in the letter). It describes that under GDUFA reauthorization for FYs 2023–2027 (the "GDUFA III Commitment Letter"), the facility may be eligible for a Post-Warning Letter Meeting to obtain preliminary FDA feedback on its corrective action plans. The footnote is anchored from the response-deadline sentence about "15 working days".
21 CFR 211.34. The section also requires the qualified consultant to perform a "comprehensive six-system audit" of the entire operation.
`https://www.fda.gov/media/86193/download` — for FDA's guidance document "Contract Manufacturing Arrangements for Drugs: Quality Agreements."

1. 21 CFR 211.113(a) — failure to establish and follow procedures to prevent objectionable microorganisms in drug products not required to be sterile. 2. 21 CFR 211.100(a) — failure to establish adequate written procedures for production and process control (process validation). 3. 21 CFR 211.192 — failure to thoroughly investigate unexplained discrepancies or batch failures. 4. 21 CFR 211.166(a) — failure to establish and follow an adequate written stability testing program.

All three lot identifiers, product names, and one numeric result are redacted as (b)(4). What is recoverable from the bullet list: - (b)(4) lot (b)(4) — TAMC: TNTC (too numerous to count); TYMC: TNTC - (b)(4) lot (b)(4) — TAMC: TNTC; TYMC: TNTC - (b)(4) lot (b)(4) — TAMC: TNTC; TYMC: (b)(4) CFU per gram Note: the third lot's TYMC value is redacted; the answer is qualified by that redaction.
- ICH Q8(R2) Pharmaceutical Development - ICH Q9(R1) Quality Risk Management - ICH Q10 Pharmaceutical Quality System

1. Sodium Sulfacetamide 8% and Sulfur 4% Topical Suspension 2. Sodium Sulfacetamide 9% and Sulfur 4% Wash 3. Sodium Sulfacetamide 9.8% Shampoo 4. Sodium Sulfacetamide 9.8% and Sulfur 4.8% Cleanser 5. Sodium Sulfacetamide 9.8% and Sulfur 4.8% Lotion 6. Sodium Sulfacetamide 9.8% and Sulfur 4.8% Cream 7. Sodium Sulfacetamide 10% and Sulfur 5% Cleanser 8. Sodium Sulfacetamide 10% Wash 9. Plexion Shampoo (sodium sulfacetamide 9.8%) 10. Plexion Lotion (sodium sulfacetamide 9.8% and sulfur 4.8%) 11. Plexion Cream (sodium sulfacetamide 9.8% and sulfur 4.8%)

1. CGMP Violation #1 (microbiological contamination): the risk assessment did not extend to all drug products where (b)(4) treatment was attempted; did not address bacterial-species identification or evidence that yeast/mold plate growth was solely bacterial. 2. CGMP Violation #2 (process validation): the firm did not evaluate the implications of changes made to product formulations. 3. CGMP Violation #3 (investigations / *Complaint Investigations*): the firm committed to revising complaint and investigation procedures but did not provide the revised procedures; did not perform a retrospective review of invalidated OOS results or address improperly invalidated results for products still on the market. 4. CGMP Violation #4 (stability): the response did not include a comprehensive review of the stability program, did not provide the revised stability protocol, and did not describe a retrospective review of batches that lack adequate stability data.
September 29 through October 16, 2025. Stated in the inspection paragraph after the salutation.
Within 15 working days after receipt of the letter. If the firm cannot complete corrective actions within 15 working days, it must state the reasons for delay and a schedule for completion.
November 6, 2025. Referenced near the start of the letter: "We reviewed your November 6, 2025 response to our Form FDA 483 in detail and acknowledge receipt of your subsequent correspondence."
04/14/2026 (April 14, 2026). Stated at the bottom of the page under "Content current as of:".
The letter is dated April 9, 2026; the page content is current as of April 14, 2026 — a difference of 5 days.

From the Conclusion paragraphs, the explicitly named possible consequences are: - Regulatory or legal action without further notice, including seizure and injunction. - Other Federal agencies may be prevented from awarding contracts to the firm. - FDA may withhold issuance of Export Certificates. - FDA may withhold approval of new applications or supplements listing the firm as a drug manufacturer. - FDA may re-inspect to verify completion of corrective actions.

The firm's CAPA included revising the drug product formulation to increase (b)(4) content and committing to no longer allow (b)(4) treatment to rework batches after failing microbiological results, and to place a reworked batch on real-time stability; the firm also submitted a risk assessment concluding no health risk for two failing lots. FDA found this inadequate because: (a) the risk assessment did not extend to all drug products where (b)(4) treatment was attempted; (b) the firm did not address product quality and patient safety risks for lots on which (b)(4) treatment was used or product released after failing micro results; and (c) the firm did not address whether additional bacterial species were present or provide evidence that growth on total yeast/mold plates was solely bacterial. FDA also notes that (b)(4) "are not a substitute for a comprehensive approach to preventing objectionable microorganisms."
"Indicated for the gradual bleaching of hyperpigmented skin conditions such as chloasma, melasma, freckles, senile lentigines and areas of melanin hyperpigmentation."

The letter states only: "We note that on (b)(4), you issued a voluntary recall of (b)(4), lots (b)(4), due to microbiological contamination." The recall date, the product name, and the affected lot identifiers are all redacted as (b)(4) — they cannot be answered from the public letter alone. The only fact recoverable is the *cause* of the recall: microbiological contamination.

The letter has two signature blocks at the close: - Francis Godwin, Director, Office of Manufacturing Quality (within Office of Compliance, CDER) — corresponds to the CGMP-violations portion (Violations #1–#4 under 21 CFR Part 211). - Tina Smith, Captain, U.S. Public Health Service, Director, Office of Unapproved Drugs & Labeling Compliance (within Office of Compliance, CDER) — corresponds to the "Unapproved New Drug Violations" section (sections 301(d) and 505(a) of the FD&C Act). The mapping is inferred from the office names: "Manufacturing Quality" → CGMP, "Unapproved Drugs & Labeling Compliance" → unapproved-new-drug labeling claims.
